likely_winner: (1) blocks this route even though (4) remains the top-level purpose
why: The owner treats source mutation and false attribution as catastrophic.
caveat: Usefulness can still be pursued through external add-on context rather than rewriting.

conflict: (4) usefulness versus a stricter misreading of (1) as forbidding any added context
why: The raw reaction condemns mutating source wording, not clearly separated external support layers.
caveat: Only valid if the added layer remains clearly external and does not rewrite the source text.

conflict: (1) untouched source text versus ordinary-reading (2) understandable on its own
likely_winner: (1) still outranks ordinary-reading (2) in the forced ranking
why: The owner says manual recovery exists and the problem is less catastrophic than silent failure.
caveat: This remains unacceptable as a library ideal even if it ranks below corruption.

conflict: (2) understandable on its own versus (1) untouched source text
why: A comfortable fragment that is misattributed is worse than a recoverably inconvenient one.
caveat: None; this is a strong owner-faithful priority edge.

scenario_name: Accurate but oversized and mentally scrambling
why: The owner says too large and not optimally subexcerpted causes chaos in the mind.
caveat: The rank is lower, but the system still needs to take this seriously because the whole point is good study conditions.

likely_winner: likely (2), but with weaker certainty than the top two ranks
why: The raw reaction lists 2 above 3, but the justification is narrower and explicitly conditional.
caveat: Tail ordering should not be overstated as fully settled.

conflict: (2) as damaged comprehensibility versus (1) untouched integrity
likely_winner: No clean winner; the two collapse toward the same catastrophic family
why: The owner says this reading of 2 becomes absolutely deadly.
caveat: This is exactly why term disambiguation is load-bearing.
